seo.title: Moethuzar | Zaw Htike Aung
seo.description: Moethuzar is a production oriented fullstack fashion ecommerce platform built as a modular monolith, combining premium storefront UX with dependable backend workflows for catalog, checkout, promotions, admin operations, customer accounts, and replay safe wishlist processing.

hero.title: Moethuzar
hero.summary: A production oriented fashion ecommerce platform built as a modular monolith, combining a premium storefront experience with dependable backend workflows for catalog, checkout, promotions, admin operations, customer accounts, and replay safe wishlist processing.
hero.intro: Moethuzar was built as a complete commerce product, not just a storefront UI. The project brought together customer facing shopping flows, operator facing admin tooling, and the backend systems needed to support real world commerce constraints such as guest identity, local payment handling, inventory logic, promotions, order operations, and asynchronous workflows. My goal was to create a product that felt refined on the surface and reliable underneath.

metadata.stack_value: Next.js, TypeScript, Prisma, PostgreSQL, Supabase Auth, Cloudflare R2, Upstash QStash

overview.label: Overview
overview.title: A fullstack commerce system, not just a storefront
overview.body:
Moethuzar was designed as a production oriented ecommerce platform with both customer and operational workflows in mind. On the frontend, it needed to feel clear, premium, and easy to navigate. On the backend, it needed to support the realities of commerce, including product variants, inventory, promotions, guest and authenticated users, manual payment flows, order tracking, and admin operations.

Rather than treating the project as a collection of pages and forms, I approached it as a full product system. That meant designing the customer experience, shaping internal tools, and building the backend architecture that made the whole platform stable, maintainable, and ready for real usage.

my_role.label: My role
my_role.title: Fullstack ownership across the product surface
my_role.body: I worked across the entire product surface, from user experience and frontend architecture to backend service design and operational workflows. This included customer facing commerce pages, authenticated account flows, admin tooling, domain modeling, checkout and order workflows, asynchronous wishlist processing, and infrastructure choices around storage, auth, and queue delivery.
items: Product and interface implementation across customer and admin surfaces
Fullstack architecture using a modular monolith approach
Domain modeling for catalog, cart, orders, payments, promotions, and wishlist
Backend service design for checkout, order creation, catalog operations, and projections
Authentication and customer account flows
Async architecture for wishlist durability and projection updates
Reliability, security, and operational safeguards such as rate limiting, request tracing, and replay or rollback aware design

problem_context.label: Context
problem_context.title: What the platform needed to solve
problem_context.body:
The challenge was not simply to build an online store. The platform needed to support the full lifecycle of a commerce product across both customer and operator needs.

On the customer side, the experience had to support browsing, product discovery, variants, cart management, checkout, promotions, accounts, and wishlist behavior. On the operations side, the system needed tools for managing catalog content, inventory, orders, payment methods, and promotions. At the same time, the backend needed to remain reliable under real product conditions, including guest flows, idempotent order creation, local payment handling, and asynchronous processing that could recover safely if delivery failed.
requirements: Customer storefront with product browsing, variants, cart, checkout, and accounts
Premium but simple user experience appropriate for fashion commerce
Admin workflows for catalog management, inventory updates, promotions, and operations
Guest and authenticated user support across shopping and account journeys
Localized checkout rules with payment and shipping constraints
Durable async processing for wishlist workflows
Stable, maintainable architecture that could evolve without collapsing into a tangled codebase

what_i_built.label: Capabilities
what_i_built.title: Organized around business capabilities rather than isolated pages
what_i_built.intro: Moethuzar was built as a full commerce product system organized around business capabilities rather than isolated pages or generic CRUD layers.

pillars.title:
Customer
storefront
pillars.body: A refined shopping environment covering product discovery, refined detail views, and seamless cart interactions. Built for clarity and a calm browsing pace.

pillars.title:
Checkout &
payment workflow
pillars.body: A mission-critical orchestration handling real-time stock verification, shipping resolution, and idempotent order creation with manual payment reconciliation.

pillars.title: Admin operations
pillars.body: Internal management surface for catalog control and promotion logic. Treated as a first-class product interface, not a utility afterthought.

pillars.title: Async wishlist
pillars.body: A replay-safe architecture built around canonical write data and queue-based delivery using QStash for production-grade reliability.

architecture.label: Architecture
architecture.title: Modular monolith with practical clean architecture boundaries
architecture.body:
I structured the backend as a domain oriented modular monolith. The goal was to keep the system cohesive and practical while still maintaining clear boundaries between transport concerns, business workflows, domain logic, and specialized persistence.

API routes stayed thin and focused on request parsing, auth or session resolution, validation, and error mapping. Services owned use case orchestration and transactional behavior. Domain modules captured important rule logic in isolation. Repositories were used selectively where persistence complexity actually justified an abstraction rather than being applied everywhere by habit.

This approach kept the codebase maintainable without overclaiming patterns that the project did not need.
principles: Requirements driven architecture rather than pattern driven architecture
Thin transport layer, rich use case services
Canonical truth first for important business domains
Selective CQRS style separation only where complexity justified it
Clear boundaries without turning the system into unnecessary abstraction

layers.title: Transport layer
layers.purpose: Request parsing, validation, auth or session context, rate limiting, delegation to services, stable API responses

layers.title: Use case and service layer
layers.purpose: Business workflow orchestration, transaction coordination, use case logic, integration across rules and data sources

layers.title: Domain rule modules
layers.purpose: Pure business logic, isolated rule modeling, deterministic logic where appropriate

layers.title: Persistence boundaries
layers.purpose: Specialized query logic, projections, and selected data access complexity where abstraction actually added value

backend_design.label: Backend design
backend_design.title: Backend workflows built for real commerce conditions
backend_design.body:
The backend was built to support real commerce workflows rather than simple database reads and writes. Important domains were modeled explicitly, transaction boundaries were treated seriously, and critical workflows were designed for reliability and recovery.

A strong example is checkout and order creation. This was not a single endpoint that saved a record. It coordinated cart validation, stock checks, pricing snapshots, promo evaluation, shipping fee resolution, payment policy selection, customer identity handling, and idempotent order creation inside a stable backend flow.

Another important backend design decision was separating durability from async delivery in the wishlist pipeline. Rather than relying on queue delivery alone, I used the database as the durable source of truth through an outbox pattern, then handled asynchronous projection updates through QStash. That created a safer, more replayable model.
highlights: Domain oriented service design rather than generic CRUD wrappers
Transactional workflows for order creation and related operations
Idempotency support through order keys and controlled write paths
Durable outbox pattern for wishlist event processing
Projection based read model for wishlist performance and resilience
Selective repository usage where read complexity or projection logic justified it
Structured error handling and request tracing for operational clarity

database_modeling.label: Database and domain modeling
database_modeling.title: A schema shaped by commerce behavior, not demo app simplicity
database_modeling.body:
The database design was one of the strongest parts of the project because it reflected real commerce behavior rather than a toy schema. The platform was modeled in PostgreSQL through Prisma with clear domain entities for customer journeys, catalog structure, order lifecycle, promotions, inventory, and asynchronous processing.

For example, inventory was modeled at the product variant level, which is the correct level for apparel commerce where size and color matter. Orders included operational detail beyond simple transaction state, such as payment status, shipping information, history, and idempotency keys. The wishlist domain separated canonical write data from a derived view model, supported by an outbox ledger for recovery and replay.
core_domains: Shipping rules and payment transfer methods
Wishlist write model, read model, and event outbox
strong_decisions: Canonical inventory lives at the variant level
Wishlist has separate write and read models
Async durability is explicitly modeled in the database
Order state captures operational reality, not just payment completion
Schema supports both customer experience and internal operations

commerce_domain_model.label: Commerce domain model
commerce_domain_model.title: Real commerce lifecycles, modeled with precision.
commerce_domain_model.body: Moving beyond simple CRUD patterns to a domain-oriented service architecture. We built a system that separates canonical write data from derived view models, ensuring that variant-level inventory and order lifecycle states remain immutable and auditable.
commerce_domain_model.subchapter_title: A schema shaped by commerce behavior

subchapter_points.title: Inventory architecture
subchapter_points.body: Variant-level inventory modeling tailored for apparel commerce, supporting complex SKU hierarchies and real-time availability locks.

subchapter_points.title: Durable ledger
subchapter_points.body: An event-based outbox pattern ensures wishlist replay safety and async read/write separation via a durable system ledger.

challenges_and_solutions.label: Challenges and solutions
challenges_and_solutions.title: Where the project became difficult
challenges_and_solutions.body: The project included several challenges that pushed it beyond simple ecommerce implementation. The most important ones were about identity, backend workflow complexity, and system stability.

items.challenge: Supporting both guest and authenticated user journeys cleanly.
items.resolution: Implementation of unified service boundaries that treat the user context as a transient property rather than a hard database divide.

items.challenge: Handling localized payment flows across varied providers.
items.resolution: Utilization of canonical database models to normalize heterogeneous provider responses into a stable internal contract.

items.challenge: Keeping wishlist updates durable without coupling reads to writes.
items.resolution: Transactional outbox records with projector-driven read models preserved eventual consistency without losing replay safety.

items.challenge: Extending admin capability without damaging maintainability.
items.resolution: Operational surfaces were treated as bounded product areas with explicit service ownership rather than generic dashboard utilities.

items.challenge: Improving account UX without redesigning the underlying auth foundations.
items.resolution: A staged interface layer was added above stable backend contracts so the customer flow could improve without destabilizing identity logic.

frontend_architecture.label: Frontend architecture
frontend_architecture.title: Server first, feature first, progressively interactive.
frontend_architecture.body:
Moethuzar utilizes the Next.js App Router to minimize client-side weight. Server Components handle route composition and data loading by default, while Client Components are strictly isolated for specific interactions like cart management and wishlist updates.

Our feature-first organization separates shared UI primitives from business-specific logic. This allowed us to build the staged account registration flow as a refined UX layer on top of stable backend contracts.

deep_dives.label: Deep dive
deep_dives.title: Checkout and order creation
deep_dives.body:
Architecture focused on rigorous backend orchestration. We ensure transactional dependability through strict cart validation, inventory integrity, and pricing snapshots within a unified transaction boundary.

Checkout was one of the most important backend workflows in the platform because it had to coordinate multiple domain rules safely. The flow had to validate the cart, confirm stock availability, resolve shipping logic, apply promotions correctly, determine payment rules by zone, and create the order in an idempotent way.

logic_points.title: State resolution
logic_points.body: Concurrent resolution of customer identity, session context, and promo evaluation before commit.

logic_points.title: Integrity guards
logic_points.body: Synchronous inventory checks and payment policy verification within the atomic transaction boundary.

logic_points.title: Snapshot persistence
logic_points.body: Capturing a point-in-time pricing and tax record to ensure auditability and long-term order accuracy.

points: Guest and signed in customer handling
Cart validation before order creation
Inventory validation before finalizing an order
Shipping fee and shipping rule resolution
Promo code evaluation and discount application
Payment policy selection based on shipping zone and method
Idempotent order creation to reduce duplicate writes and unsafe retries
deep_dives.why_it_matters: This flow shows that the project was built as a real system, not just a storefront interface. It demonstrates backend orchestration, transactional thinking, and careful handling of business logic under conditions that matter in production.

deep_dives.label: Deep dive
deep_dives.title: Replay safe wishlist architecture
deep_dives.body:
Leveraging a transactional outbox pattern to achieve absolute eventual consistency. User interactions are committed as durable outbox events within the same transaction as the canonical write model.

The wishlist pipeline was one of the strongest architecture stories in the codebase because it moved beyond simple CRUD. When wishlist writes occur, the system updates canonical data and records a durable outbox event in the same transactional boundary. Delivery to the projection path is then handled asynchronously through QStash, while the derived view can still be replayed safely from the canonical source.

logic_points.title: Transactional outbox
logic_points.body: Ensures atomicity between business state and event propagation.

logic_points.title: Replay safety
logic_points.body: Decoupled read models allow for full state projection at any point.

points: WishlistItem as canonical write model
WishlistItemView as derived read model
EventOutbox as durable async ledger
QStash as delivery mechanism
Projector service designed to be idempotent
Rebuild and replay path retained for recovery
deep_dives.why_it_matters: This system separated durability from delivery, which is a much stronger production approach than assuming the queue alone is the source of truth. It shows system design maturity, async workflow thinking, and operational awareness.

deep_dives.label: Deep dive
deep_dives.title: Admin tools as product surfaces
deep_dives.body:
Internal tooling designed with the same performance and aesthetic rigor as the primary storefront.

We build bespoke administration interfaces that move beyond generic SaaS templates. This custom cockpit gives curators direct control over complex business logic.
points: Catalog and product variant management
Inventory updates and visibility
Promo management
Transfer method and payment configuration
Dashboard and operations support

logic_points.title: Observation
logic_points.body: Real-time telemetry for monitoring checkout throughput.

logic_points.title: Governance
logic_points.body: Role-based access control and audit logs.

deep_dives.why_it_matters: This broadens the project from a frontend storefront into a real fullstack product. It also shows that I can design for internal operators, not only customer facing UI.

engineering_principles.label: Engineering principles
engineering_principles.title: How the system was shaped
engineering_principles.body: I did not try to force one architectural style across the entire system. Instead, I used stronger patterns where the domain actually demanded them and kept simpler flows straightforward where complexity was lower.
items: Canonical truth over duplicated state
Patterns only where complexity justifies them
Thin transport, rich use case services
Server first rendering with isolated client interactivity
Operational reliability designed in, not bolted on
UX upgrades without destabilizing backend contracts
Internal tools treated as product surfaces, not afterthoughts

delivery_methodology.label: Delivery methodology
delivery_methodology.title: How the work moved forward
delivery_methodology.body: This project was developed as a set of vertical slices rather than as separate frontend and backend tracks. That kept product features coherent and helped ensure that interface decisions and operational workflows evolved together.

items.title: Vertical slice delivery by business capability
items.body: Focusing on delivering end-to-end features rather than technical layers, ensuring value is visible early.

items.title: Risk first design for critical workflows
items.body: Tackling the most complex and high-risk integrations first to stabilize the core architecture.

items.title: Documentation backed implementation
items.body: Maintaining live architectural documents and QA notes to guide implementation as the system scaled.

items.title: Business critical testing over shallow coverage
items.body: Deeply testing service logic and security-sensitive flows instead of superficial metric chasing.

items.title: Rollback and replay paths preserved
items.body: Designing asynchronous features with built-in recovery paths and operational visibility.

reliability_security.label: Reliability and security
reliability_security.title: Trust built into the flows
reliability_security.body: One of the most important goals in this project was making the backend trustworthy, not just functional. That meant designing critical workflows with clear boundaries, handling abusive or duplicate behavior carefully, and keeping recovery paths available for asynchronous features.
security_points: Abuse controls around account precheck flows
reliability_points: Durable outbox pattern for async events
Rebuild path for derived wishlist state
Structured error envelopes and request IDs
reliability_security.closing: These are the details that separate a polished demo from a system that was designed with real usage in mind.

key_challenges.label: Key challenges
key_challenges.title: What made the project complex
key_challenges.body: The project included several challenges that pushed it beyond simple ecommerce implementation. The most important ones were about identity, backend workflow complexity, operational flexibility, and maintaining system stability while evolving UX.
items: Supporting both guest and authenticated user journeys cleanly
Handling localized payment flows and manual verification constraints
Designing a replay safe async wishlist pipeline
Extending admin capability without damaging maintainability
Improving authentication and registration UX without redesigning auth foundations

solutions.label: Solutions
solutions.title: How I approached the hard parts
solutions.body: I approached each challenge by treating the system as a product with both experience and operational layers. Some solutions were architectural, some were domain modeling choices, and some were interface decisions built on top of stable contracts.
items: Clear service boundaries for checkout, order, catalog, and wishlist domains
Canonical database models for critical truth sources
Durable outbox and projector design for wishlist processing
Feature first frontend architecture with server first rendering
Admin tooling designed as a first class part of the platform
UX refinement layered onto existing auth foundations rather than destabilizing them
Operational guardrails such as rate limiting, request tracing, and replay planning

outcome.label: Outcome
outcome.title: A production oriented fullstack commerce platform
outcome.body:
Moethuzar became a production oriented fullstack commerce platform with broad feature coverage, stable architectural foundations, and room for future growth. The final product combined customer storefront UX, admin operations, backend workflows, and asynchronous reliability in one coherent codebase.

Even without public scale metrics, the project demonstrates real fullstack depth. It shows customer experience design, backend orchestration, domain modeling, internal tooling, asynchronous processing, and production minded reliability working together in one system.
points: Customer, admin, and operational workflows designed in one coherent platform
Stable domain model for catalog, checkout, orders, promotions, and wishlist
Strong backend depth through checkout, idempotency, and async pipeline design
Internal tools treated as serious product surfaces
Maintainable fullstack foundation for future evolution

learnings.label: What I learned
learnings.title: What this project sharpened for me
learnings.body:
This project reinforced that architecture should follow domain complexity, not fashion. Simple flows should stay simple. Complex flows should get the structure they need. It also reinforced that durability and delivery should not be treated as the same thing, especially once asynchronous workflows enter the product.

On the frontend side, it confirmed that good UX changes do not always require backend rewrites if the underlying contracts are sound. On the product side, it strengthened my belief that internal tools deserve the same care as customer facing experiences.
items: Architecture should follow domain complexity, not trends
Durability and delivery should be separated in async systems
Internal tools are part of the product, not support debris
Strong UX evolution depends on stable backend foundations
Fullstack quality comes from joining surface experience and system reliability

final_frame.label: Final frame
final_frame.title: Considered on the surface. Dependable underneath.

next_project.label: Next project
next_project.title: Continue to the next case study

technical_claims.title: Core technical claims
items: Built a modular monolith ecommerce platform with Next.js, TypeScript, Prisma, and PostgreSQL
Designed customer, admin, and operational workflows in one coherent codebase
Modeled apparel commerce at the variant level with inventory, pricing, and imagery support
Built checkout with stock validation, shipping resolution, promo application, payment policy selection, and idempotent order creation
Implemented guest and authenticated account journeys, including wishlist merge behavior
Designed a replay safe async wishlist pipeline using durable outbox records, projection models, and QStash delivery
Built admin facing catalog and operations capabilities alongside the storefront
Applied rate limiting, structured errors, request tracing, and rollback aware delivery design
Used Server Components by default and isolated client interactivity where needed
